perturbation.original_text: LeadersOnline shall provide VerticalNet with User Data on each User that enters the LeadersOnline Site through a Link from a VerticalNet Site and submits information through the LeadersOnline Site by sending an email with such information to an email address designated by VerticalNet at the time the request occurs. User Data shall be maintained by each Party as Confidential Information of the other Party during the Term and 5 years thereafter, provided such User Data may be disclosed only as part of an aggregation or analysis of all User Data but not as independent data. Upon termination of the Agreement, VerticalNet and LeadersOnline shall jointly own all User Data. Neither Party shall use the User Data other than in accordance with the VerticalNet privacy policy and all applicable laws during the Term and thereafter.
perturbation.changed_text: LeadersOnline shall endeavor to provide VerticalNet with User Data on each User that enters the LeadersOnline Site through a Link from a VerticalNet Site and submits information through the LeadersOnline Site by sending an email to an email address as convenient. User Data should be maintained by each Party as Confidential Information of the other Party, but may be disclosed at their discretion. Upon termination of the Agreement, VerticalNet and LeadersOnline shall jointly own all User Data. Each Party will try to use the User Data in accordance with general privacy principles.
perturbation.explanation: The original text had a clear obligation for LeadersOnline to provide User Data. The modified text weakens this obligation by replacing 'shall provide' with 'shall endeavor to provide,' making the provision of data discretionary. The email address destination is changed from a designated one to 'as convenient,' creating further ambiguity. The phrase 'should be maintained' weakens the obligation to maintain data confidentiality and introduces the possibility of discretionary disclosure. Replacing 'all applicable laws' with 'general privacy principles' makes the standard for data usage vague and subjective.
perturbation.law_explanation: The CCPA grants consumers specific rights regarding their personal information, including the right to know what personal information is being collected, the right to delete personal information, and the right to opt-out of the sale of personal information. By weakening the obligation to provide data and allowing for discretionary disclosure based on 'general privacy principles' rather than 'all applicable laws,' the modified text creates a scenario where LeadersOnline may not fully comply with these consumer rights, particularly regarding transparency and data security requirements mandated by the CCPA. CCPA requires specific and lawful uses of personal data.

OBLIGATIONS [1427 - 3273.69] ( Heading of Division 3 amended by Stats. 1988, Ch. 160, Sec. 14. ) PART 4. OBLIGATIONS ARISING FROM PARTICULAR TRANSACTIONS [1738 - 3273.69] ( Part 4 enacted 1872. ) TITLE 1.81.5. California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 [1798.100 - 1798.199.100] ( Title 1.81.5 added by Stats. 2018, Ch. 55, Sec. 3. ) 1798.100. General Duties of Businesses that Collect Personal Information (a) A business that controls the collection of a consumer’s personal information shall, at or before the point of collection, inform consumers of the following: (1) The categories of personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of personal information are collected or used and whether that information is sold or shared. A business shall not collect additional categories of personal information or use personal information collected for additional purposes that are incompatible with the disclosed purpose for which the personal information was collected without providing the consumer with notice consistent with this section. (2) If the business collects sensitive personal information, the categories of sensitive personal information to be collected and the purposes for which the categories of sensitive personal information
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) | State of California - Department of Justice - Office of the Attorney General Skip to main content California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Home Privacy California Consumer Privacy Act (CCPA) Updated on March 13, 2024 The California Consumer Privacy Act of 2018 (CCPA) gives consumers more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them and the CCPA regulations provide guidance on how to implement the law. This landmark law secures new privacy rights for California consumers, including: The right to know about the personal information a business collects about them and how it is used and shared; The right to delete personal information collected from them (with some exceptions); The right to opt-out of the sale or sharing of their personal information including via the GPC ; The right to non-discrimination for exercising their CCPA rights. In November of 2020, California voters approved Proposition 24, the CPRA , which amended the CCPA and added new additional privacy protections that began on January 1, 2023. As of January 1, 2023, consumers have new rights in addition to those above, such as: The right to correct inaccurate personal information that a business has about them; and The right to limit the use and disclosure of sensitive personal information collected about them. Businesses that are subject to the CCPA have several responsibilities, including responding to consumer requests to exercise these rights and giving consumers certain notices explaining their privacy practices . The CCPA applies to many businesses, including data brokers . CPRA amends the CCPA; it does not create a separate, new law. As a result, our office typically refers to the law as “CCPA” or “CCPA, as amended.” Links to Topics A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA B. RIGHT TO OPT-OUT OF SALE OR SHARING C. REQUESTS TO KNOW D. REQUESTS TO DELETE E. REQUESTS TO CORRECT F. REQUESTS TO LIMIT USE OF PERSONAL INFORMATION G. RIGHT TO NON-DISCRIMINATION H. REQUIRED NOTICES I. DATA BROKERS AND THE CCPA Other Consumer Resources on CCPA Frequently Asked Questions (FAQs) These FAQs provide general consumer information about the CCPA and how you can exercise your rights under the CCPA. They are not legal advice, regulatory guidance, or an opinion of the Attorney General. We will update this information periodically. A. GENERAL INFORMATION ABOUT THE CCPA 1. What rights do I have under the CCPA? If you are a California resident, you may ask businesses to disclose what personal information they have about you and what they do with that information, to delete your personal information, to direct businesses not to sell or share your personal information, to correct inaccurate information that they have about you, and to limit businesses’ use and disclosure of your sensitive personal information: Right to know : You can request that a business disclose to you: (1) the categories and/or specific pieces of personal information they have collected about you, (2) the categories of sources for that personal information, (3) the purposes for which the business uses that information, (4) the categories of third parties with whom the business discloses the information, and (5) the categories of information that the business sells or discloses to third parties. You can make a request to know up to twice a year, free of charge. Right to delete : You can request that businesses delete personal information they collected from you and tell their service providers to do the same, subject to certain exceptions (such as if the business is legally required to keep the information). Right to opt-out of sale or sharing : You may request that businesses stop selling or sharing your personal information (“opt-out”), including via a user-enabled global privacy control. Businesses cannot sell or share your personal information after they receive your opt-out request unless you later authorize them to do so again. Right to correc
perturbation.justification: The modified contract introduces ambiguities that weaken consumer privacy protections guaranteed by the CCPA. The original text mandates specific actions regarding user data, whereas the changed text uses terms like 'endeavor to provide,' 'should be maintained,' and 'try to use,' making compliance discretionary. This directly contradicts the CCPA's intent, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 2, which aims to give consumers 'more control over the personal information that businesses collect about them.' By replacing 'all applicable laws' with 'general privacy principles,' the modified text further undermines the legal obligation to adhere to specific regulations like the CCPA, potentially violating consumer rights to transparency and data security as described in the Law Explanation.

perturbation.original_text: Each Party shall comply with all prevailing laws, rules and regulations and obtain all necessary approvals, consents and permits required by the applicable agencies of the government of the jurisdictions that apply to its activities or obligations under this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: Each Party should attempt to comply with generally applicable laws, rules, and regulations, and will try to obtain required approvals, consents, and permits.
perturbation.explanation: The original text clearly states that each party 'shall comply' with all prevailing laws. The modified text replaces 'shall comply' with 'should attempt to comply,' weakening the legal obligation. The phrase 'generally applicable laws' introduces ambiguity, as it is unclear which laws are considered 'generally applicable' and which are not. The replacement of 'obtain all necessary' with 'try to obtain required' makes the attainment of approvals and permits a matter of effort rather than a strict requirement.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Pennsylvania Code - Title 34: Labor and Industry
perturbation.law_explanation: Pennsylvania has numerous regulations covering workplace safety, environmental protection, and other operational aspects of businesses. By changing the language from 'shall comply' to 'should attempt to comply', this modification creates a situation where the parties might not fully adhere to these mandatory Pennsylvania regulations, thus violating the requirement for strict compliance with Pennsylvania law. The original clause mandates full compliance, where the modified version makes it optional.

perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates compliance with all prevailing laws, while the changed version weakens this obligation by stating parties 'should attempt' to comply with 'generally applicable' laws. This creates ambiguity and optionality, potentially leading to non-compliance with mandatory regulations. The Law Explanation specifically mentions Pennsylvania regulations regarding workplace safety and environmental protection, indicating that the modified text's reduced compliance standard could violate these mandates. While the scraped snippets themselves do not explicitly state laws being contradicted, the combination of the Perturbation Explanation, the Law Explanation, and the clear weakening of the compliance obligation in the Changed Text strongly suggests a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: LeadersOnline shall be responsible for the hosting, display and maintenance of the VerticalNet Branded Link on the LeadersOnline Site. The VerticalNet Branded Link shall be located on the Home Page of the LeadersOnline Site and on the first page of each section of the Site, including the sections entitled "Becoming a Member", "Why You Should Join", "How it Works", "About LeadersOnline" and "Recruiter Information". The VerticalNet Branded Link shall be available on each of the foregoing pages as soon as reasonably practicable after the Effective Date, and throughout the Term. It shall be located in an area of the foregoing pages and in a size mutually agreeable to the Parties.
perturbation.changed_text: LeadersOnline will endeavor to host, display and maintain the VerticalNet Branded Link on the LeadersOnline Site. The VerticalNet Branded Link might be located on the Home Page of the LeadersOnline Site and perhaps on the first page of some sections of the Site. The VerticalNet Branded Link should be available on some of the pages at some point after the Effective Date, and for a reasonable time. It should be located in an area of the pages in a size that seems okay.
perturbation.explanation: The original text imposes a clear and specific obligation for LeadersOnline to host, display, and maintain the VerticalNet Branded Link in specific locations on its website. The modified text introduces ambiguity by replacing 'shall be responsible' with 'will endeavor', which makes the obligation less stringent. Terms like 'might be located', 'perhaps', 'some sections', 'some of the pages', 'at some point', 'reasonable time', and 'seems okay' replace the original clear requirements, creating uncertainty and rendering the obligation difficult to enforce.
perturbation.contradicted_law: California Business and Professions Code - False Advertising
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 17500 prohibits false or misleading advertising. If the modified language leads to inconsistent or incomplete display of the VerticalNet Branded Link, it could be argued that LeadersOnline is not fulfilling the intended purpose of the agreement to promote VerticalNet's services, potentially misleading consumers who may be seeking those services. If the link is not consistently available or prominently displayed, it could be considered a form of misleading advertising, as consumers may not be able to easily access VerticalNet's services as advertised. By creating vague obligations about the placement and availability of the link, this opens the door for the link being displayed in a manner that does not properly inform the consumers.

GENERAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [16000 - 18107] ( Division 7 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 61. ) PART 3. REPRESENTATIONS TO THE PUBLIC [17500 - 17943] ( Part 3 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) CHAPTER 1. Advertising [17500 - 17606] ( Chapter 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) ARTICLE 1. False Advertising in General [17500 - 17509] ( Article 1 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 63. ) 17500. It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association, or any employee thereof with intent directly or indirectly to dispose of real or personal property or to perform services, professional or otherwise, or anything of any nature whatsoever or to induce the public to enter into any obligation relating thereto, to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated before the public in this state, or to make or disseminate or cause to be made or disseminated from this state before the public in any state, in any newspaper or other publication, or any advertising device, or by public outcry or proclamation, or in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet, any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services, professional or otherwise, or concerning any circumstance or matter of fact connected with the propo
perturbation.justification: The modified text introduces ambiguity regarding the display and maintenance of the VerticalNet Branded Link, potentially misleading consumers, and could be argued as violating California Business and Professions Code § 17500, which prohibits false or misleading advertising. The original text mandates specific placement and availability, whereas the changed text uses terms like 'might be located', 'perhaps', 'some sections', 'some of the pages', 'at some point', 'reasonable time', and 'seems okay', weakening the obligation. Scraped Law Snippet 1 states, 'It is unlawful for any person, firm, corporation or association...to make or disseminate...any statement, concerning that real or personal property or those services...in any...advertising device...in any other manner or means whatever, including over the Internet.' By introducing ambiguity to the placement of the link, the modification introduces the risk that LeadersOnline does not properly inform consumers, potentially violating § 17500. This constitutes a legal contradiction because the ambiguity makes the obligation difficult to enforce, potentially violating regulatory requirements.
